about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: Born beside the fortress walls, built on 15 years of bay-road knowledge.
about.seoDesc: A Kotor family business since 2007. We grew up in the shadow of San Giovanni, know every hairpin on the Lovcen serpentine, and greet every flight at Tivat arrivals.
about.p1: Since 2007, Kotor Car Hire has been connecting travellers with reliable, affordable rental cars in the Bay of Kotor. What started as a simple idea — making it easier for visitors to explore this UNESCO-listed corner of the Adriatic — has grown into one of the region's most trusted car rental services.
about.p2: The Bay of Kotor is best experienced at your own pace. The winding bay road, hidden mountain villages, and medieval towns are difficult to reach by public transport. We saw an opportunity to give travellers the freedom to discover Kotor and its surroundings on their own terms — no tour bus schedules, no missed viewpoints, no compromises.
about.p3a: We provide car rental services throughout
about.p3d: , and locations across the coast and interior.
about.promise1: Full insurance with zero excess options available
about.promise2: Tivat Airport meet-and-greet — we bring the car to you at arrivals
about.promise3: No hidden fees — the price you see is the price you pay
about.promise4: Cross-border travel permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
about.localText: Based in Kotor, we know every road around the bay — the best routes, the hidden gems, and the shortcuts that guidebooks miss. Whether you're driving from
about.localMid2: UNESCO Old Town, taking the scenic bay road to
about.localEnd: , or heading up the serpentine to Lovcen National Park, we can help you plan the perfect road trip.
about.contactText: Have a question about renting a car in Kotor? We're available via phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Search our fleet online and book in minutes — or reach out and we'll help you find the perfect car for your trip.

kotor.title: Car Hire in Kotor
kotor.subtitle: Park beneath 4.5 km of fortress walls and explore twelve centuries of history on foot.
kotor.seoDesc: 1,355 steps to San Giovanni, St Tryphon Cathedral since 1166, and 4.5 km of Venetian walls. Park outside the Sea Gate and let the Old Town unfold.
kotor.p1: Kotor is one of the best-preserved medieval towns on the Adriatic, sitting at the head of a dramatic fjord-like bay. The UNESCO-listed Old Town is a maze of narrow streets, Venetian palaces, Romanesque churches, and lively squares. Above it all, the fortress of San Giovanni climbs 1,200 steps to offer sweeping views across the bay.
kotor.p2b: gives you the freedom to explore the entire bay and beyond. Tivat Airport is just 8 km away — a 15-minute drive. Parking is available outside the Old Town walls, with larger lots on the approach roads.
kotor.stJohnText: The fortifications above Kotor date back to the 6th century and were expanded by the Venetians. The 1,200-step climb to the top rewards with panoramic views of the bay, Old Town rooftops, and the surrounding mountains. Entry is free outside peak season.
kotor.cathedralText: Built in 809 AD and reconstructed in 1124, the Cathedral of St. Tryphon is one of the finest Romanesque buildings on the Adriatic coast. The twin bell towers are Kotor's most recognisable landmark, and the interior houses a collection of medieval gold and silverwork.
kotor.ladyOfRocksTitle: Our Lady of the Rocks, Perast
kotor.ladyOfRocksText2: , this church sits on a man-made island in the Bay of Kotor. Since 1452, locals have thrown rocks into the bay to enlarge the island. Ferry boats run from Perast harbour for a few euros. The drive from Kotor takes 15 minutes along the bay road.
kotor.cetinjeText: Built in 1701, the monastery houses relics including the hand of John the Baptist. The drive from Kotor takes about an hour through the scenic mountain road via Njegos Mausoleum and Lovcen National Park.
kotor.royalCourtText: Montenegro's royal palace from 1871, located in the former royal capital. Displays art, artefacts, and photographs from the country's royal history.
kotor.routeDifficulty: Moderate — narrow streets in Old Town, standard roads approaching
kotor.routeVehicle: Compact car (easier parking near Old Town walls)
kotor.routeParking: Limited in Old Town. Use car parks on approach roads (€1-2/hour)
kotor.routeDistances: From Tivat Airport: 15 minutes · From Budva: 30 minutes

tivat-airport.title: Tivat Airport Car Hire
tivat-airport.subtitle: Land between mountains and bay. Keys in hand before your luggage cools.
tivat-airport.seoDesc: TIV sits 8 km from Kotor's fortress walls. We meet every arrival, hand over the keys in the terminal, and you drive the bay road to the Old Town in fifteen minutes flat.

budva.title: Car Hire in Budva
budva.subtitle: Twenty-five centuries of settlement, 21 km of riviera sand, and a tunnel ride from Kotor.
budva.seoDesc: Through the Sozina tunnel from Kotor in 30 minutes. Mogren's cliff-cut coves, Jaz festival beach, and a 2,500-year-old citadel that turns into a nightlife strip after dark.

perast.title: Car Hire in Perast
perast.subtitle: Sixteen palaces, 350 residents, and an island built by hand since 1452.
perast.seoDesc: Fifteen minutes from Kotor's walls: Perast's Venetian waterfront, the Buijovich Palace museum, and a five-minute boat ride to the island church locals have enlarged with thrown stones for over 500 years.

podgorica.title: Car Hire in Podgorica
podgorica.subtitle: Six rivers, three eras of architecture, and the gateway to Montenegro's mountainous interior.
podgorica.seoDesc: Cross the Lovćen massif from Kotor in 90 minutes. Podgorica sits at the meeting of six rivers — Ottoman clock towers beside brutalist blocks beside glass towers. From here, Lake Skadar and Durmitor are within reach.

podgorica-airport.title: Podgorica Airport Car Hire
podgorica-airport.subtitle: Montenegro's year-round hub. Code TGD, named after a city that no longer exists.
podgorica-airport.seoDesc: Podgorica Airport (TGD) has the widest winter flight network in Montenegro. Collect at arrivals and drive 90 minutes through mountain scenery to Kotor's medieval bay.

dubrovnik-airport.title: Dubrovnik Airport Car Hire
dubrovnik-airport.subtitle: Land in Croatia, reach Kotor's walls in two hours via one of Europe's great coastal roads.
dubrovnik-airport.seoDesc: Dubrovnik (DBV) has the flights; Kotor has the bay. A two-hour E65 coast drive with one border crossing, a Green Card in the glovebox, and a full tank of fuel.

tivat.title: Car Hire in Tivat
tivat.subtitle: From Cold War naval arsenal to Adriatic superyacht marina in two decades.
tivat.seoDesc: Once a Yugoslav navy base, now home to Porto Montenegro and 450 berths of floating luxury. Collect your car at Tivat Airport and reach Kotor's medieval walls in 20 minutes.

montenegro.title: Montenegro Driving Guide
montenegro.subtitle: Bay roads, mountain switchbacks, and border crossings — from a driver who has done them all from Kotor.
montenegro.seoDesc: 25 hairpin bends on the Lovćen serpentine, ferry shortcuts across the bay, zero-tolerance drink-driving, and when to avoid the cruise-ship parking crush. A local's road guide.

border-crossing.title: Balkans Border Crossing Guide
border-crossing.subtitle: Five borders within driving range of Kotor. Here is what each one requires.
border-crossing.seoDesc: Green Card insurance, original rental contracts, and realistic queue times at Debeli Brijeg, Sukobin, Šćepan Polje, Dobrakovo, and Kula. Practical advice from Kotor outward.

airportPickup.desc: Collect your car at Tivat Airport, just 8 km from Kotor. No shuttle, no waiting.

fullInsurance.desc: Comprehensive coverage with zero excess option. Drive the Bay of Kotor with peace of mind.

support.title: 24/7 Support
support.desc: Roadside assistance and customer service available around the clock, every day.

noHiddenFees.title: No Hidden Fees
noHiddenFees.desc: Fuel policy, mileage, tax — everything is included in the price you see.

freeCancellation.title: Free Cancellation
freeCancellation.desc: Plans change. Cancel up to 24 hours before pickup for a full refund.

crossBorder.title: Cross-Border
crossBorder.desc: Drive into Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, or Kosovo. Cross-border travel permitted.

0.text: Picked up a Polo at Tivat Airport and were in Kotor Old Town within 15 minutes. Spent the week driving to Perast, Budva, and up to Lovcen. Fantastic service.

1.text: The bay road from Kotor to Perast was the highlight of our holiday. Having our own car made it easy to stop at every viewpoint. Green card for Croatia was ready at pickup.

2.text: We drove from Kotor to Dubrovnik and back in a day, then explored Lovcen National Park the next. The Jeep handled the mountain roads with no issues at all.

0.a: A valid driving licence (must show Latin script details), a foreign passport, and a physical credit card in the main driver's name. International Driving Permit recommended if your licence is only valid in its country of origin. Copies or electronic licences are not accepted.

1.q: Can I drive to Croatia or Albania?
1.a: Yes! If you plan to travel outside Montenegro, notify the rental agent in advance so they can complete the required documents. Cross-border travel to neighbouring countries is permitted.

2.a: The minimum age and driving experience requirements vary by vehicle. These details are shown when you search for available cars.

3.a: All vehicles come with insurance — most include Third Party Liability and Collision Damage Waiver (CDW) in the base price. VAT, equipment kits (first aid, warning triangle, visibility vest), and a transparent fuel policy are also included. Mileage terms vary by vehicle. Extended coverage options are available for an additional fee.

4.q: How does pickup work at Tivat Airport?
4.a: Choose your pickup time during booking. The agent will meet you at Tivat Airport arrivals with your name on a sign. Your car is parked right outside. Total time from landing to driving into Kotor: about 15 minutes.

5.q: Can I drop off at a different location?
5.a: Yes — one-way rentals are supported across 28 cities and towns in Montenegro. Simply choose different pickup and dropoff locations during booking.

6.a: Free cancellation up to 7 days before the rental start date. For luxury cars and convertibles, free cancellation is available up to 30 days before. A 6% payment service fee applies to all cancellations.

7.a: Deposit amounts vary by vehicle — some cars are available with no deposit. If required, the deposit must be on a physical credit card in the main driver's name and is refunded upon return of the vehicle in good condition.

8.q: What happens if I exceed the mileage limit?
8.a: Vehicles with mileage restrictions are charged per kilometre over the limit. Rates are shown in the vehicle details before you book.

9.a: No. The price you see includes all taxes and fees. What's shown at booking is what you pay.

10.q: How much do I pay upfront?
10.a: Only 15–20% of the total is required to confirm your booking. The remainder is paid directly to the rental agent at pickup.

11.q: Can I rent without a credit card?
11.a: Some vehicles are available without a credit card. Check availability when searching — card-free options are clearly marked.

aboutBody.h1: A Family Business Inside the Fortress Walls
aboutBody.p1: Our story begins where the Skurda River meets the bay, a few hundred metres from Kotor's Sea Gate. In 2007, two brothers who grew up climbing the 1,355 steps to San Giovanni fortress decided that visitors deserved the same freedom to roam. Fifteen years later, Kotor Car Hire places keys in the hands of thousands of travellers each season — from the arrivals hall at Tivat to the cobblestones outside St Tryphon Cathedral.
aboutBody.h2: Why This Bay Demands a Car
aboutBody.p2: Public buses trace the main highway and stop. Full stop. They will not detour to the unmarked viewpoint above Perast where the bay opens into two arms. They cannot pause at the Roman mosaics in Risan or navigate the single-track lane to Gornji Stoliv, the abandoned village clinging to the cliff face 400 metres above the water. Only a steering wheel and a sense of curiosity unlock Boka Kotorska properly.
aboutBody.p3: Collection points span the bay: Tivat Airport (8 km from Kotor's walls), directly outside the River Gate car park, and across 28 towns from Herceg Novi to Ulcinj. Economy hatchbacks slip through Old Town approach roads with ease. SUVs handle the Lovcen serpentine's 25 hairpin bends without complaint. Every vehicle carries full insurance, and cross-border documentation for Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo can be arranged at booking.
aboutBody.promise1: Comprehensive insurance with a zero-excess option on every booking
aboutBody.promise2: A named driver waiting at Tivat arrivals, sign in hand, car parked outside
aboutBody.promise3: Transparent pricing — no airport surcharges, no fuel-policy surprises
aboutBody.promise4: Green Card paperwork prepared for five neighbouring countries
aboutBody.promise5: Free cancellation up to seven days before collection
aboutBody.promise6: Around-the-clock roadside support, Kotor to Zabljak and everywhere between
aboutBody.p4: Ask us which mornings the cruise ships dock and choke the Old Town car parks. We will tell you to arrive before 07:30 or skip to Dobrota instead. Ask about the Vrmac ridge trail and we will explain exactly where to leave the car at the trailhead near Prčanj. From the timing of the Kamenari-Lepetane ferry to the quietest hour at the Debeli Brijeg border crossing, our advice comes from driving these roads daily — not from a guidebook reprint.
aboutBody.p5: WhatsApp, Viber, old-fashioned phone calls — pick whatever suits you. We reply quickly, even during the frantic July cruise-ship season. Browse the fleet online, lock in a price, and we handle everything else. Or simply message us a question about parking near the Cathedral, the best road to Cetinje, or whether that mountain pass is open in December.

kotorBody.p1: Fortifications first rose above Kotor in the 6th century, but the town's documented history stretches further — Roman settlement, Byzantine rule, nearly four hundred years under Venice. UNESCO inscribed the entire Natural and Culturo-Historical Region of Kotor in 1979, recognising not just the walled Old Town but the surrounding bay landscape as a single cultural entity. Walk through the Sea Gate (Vrata od Mora), built in 1555, and you step into a medieval street plan that has barely shifted since.
kotorBody.p2: Parking options line the approach from Tivat Airport, 8 km and roughly fifteen minutes away. The largest lot sits below the walls near the River Gate; a second, less crowded option is the Dobrota strip heading north. On days when cruise liners are in port — sometimes three or four at once between May and October — arrive before 07:30 or expect a scramble for spaces.
kotorBody.landmarksTitle: Inside the Walls and Above Them
kotorBody.stJohnText: Precisely 1,355 steps lead from the Old Town's northern edge to the fortress summit at 280 metres. Venetian engineers reinforced the 4.5 km curtain wall between the 15th and 18th centuries, stitching together earlier Byzantine stonework with their own bastions. Bring water, wear proper shoes, and budget forty-five minutes upward. The view from the top — the inner bay splitting into two channels around the Vrmac peninsula — is worth every step. Outside July and August, entry is free.
kotorBody.cathedralText: A church has stood on this site since 809 AD, but the present Romanesque structure dates from 1166, making it one of two Roman Catholic cathedrals in Montenegro. The twin bell towers, rebuilt after the 1667 earthquake, define Kotor's skyline. Inside, a ciborium over the main altar shelters 14th-century frescoes, and the reliquary treasury holds gold and silver pieces spanning five centuries. St Tryphon, the city's patron, is celebrated each 3 February with processions through the squares.
kotorBody.perastTrip: Fifteen minutes north along the bay road, Perast sits in silence compared to Kotor's bustle. Leave the car on the waterfront, board a skiff to Our Lady of the Rocks — the island locals have been enlarging with thrown stones since 1452 — and return for coffee at a harbourside palazzo. The drive itself is one of the finest short stretches of road in the country, curving along the waterline beneath karst cliffs.
kotorBody.cetinjeTrip: An hour of switchbacks through Lovcen National Park deposits you in Cetinje, Montenegro's old royal capital. The monastery shelters relics said to include the hand of John the Baptist. Continue upward to the Njegos Mausoleum, perched at 1,657 metres, where four countries are visible on a clear morning.
kotorBody.royalCourtText: Also in Cetinje, the 1871 Royal Court — once the palace of King Nikola I — preserves paintings, weapons, and photographs from the brief era of Montenegrin monarchy. It stands beside the monastery on a tree-lined boulevard in the former capital.

budvaBody.h1: Where 2,500 Years of History Meets 21 km of Sand
budvaBody.p1: Greeks from Epidaurus founded a settlement here around the 5th century BC. Romans came next, then Slavic tribes, Venetians, Austrians, and finally modern Montenegro. Each left a mark, but Budva today is defined by contrast: a walled citadel on a finger of rock jutting into the Adriatic, flanked on both sides by beach bars pumping music until dawn. It is not subtle, and it does not try to be.
budvaBody.p2: Driving from Kotor takes roughly thirty minutes through a mountain tunnel that cuts beneath the Lovćen foothills. From Tivat Airport, the road south skirts the bay before climbing briefly and descending to the Budva riviera. A rental car transforms the coast: Mogren is five minutes away, Jaz ten, and Sveti Stefan fifteen — distances that feel considerable without wheels but trivial with them.
budvaBody.beachesIntro: Budva's municipality claims 21 kilometres of coastline. Four beaches stand out:
budvaBody.mogren: A pair of pebble coves linked by a tunnel blasted through the cliffs. Walking distance from the Old Town, yet feels secluded once you pass through the rock
budvaBody.jaz: The largest sandy stretch near Budva, backed by a shallow lagoon. Hosts the Sea Dance festival each summer and fills with families the rest of the time
budvaBody.svetiStefan: Visible from the Adriatic Highway as a photogenic fortified islet. The mainland Queen's Beach is open to all; the island itself is a private resort
budvaBody.becici: A 2 km arc of fine sand that European travel writers once voted the continent's finest beach. Still popular, less frantic than Budva's main strip
budvaBody.oldTownText: Budva's Stari Grad occupies a compact peninsula fortified since antiquity. Cobbled lanes barely two metres wide thread between Venetian-era townhouses, 7th-century churches, and a scatter of restaurants that spill chairs onto every available paving stone. At the southern tip, the Citadela fortress — now a summer events venue — commands views along the coast in both directions. A small library inside holds editions in a dozen languages, left by visiting sailors over the centuries.
budvaBody.svetiStefanTrip: Six kilometres south on the highway, the road above Sveti Stefan offers a camera angle that has sold a million postcards. Below, the mainland beach is free and open to all. A car lets you arrive early, before the tour coaches from Budva claim every parking space.
budvaBody.kotorTrip: North through the tunnel: Kotor's UNESCO-listed Old Town in thirty minutes. Most visitors to Budva make this trip at least once, and many decide they should have based themselves in Kotor instead — a good argument for having a car.
budvaBody.lovcenTrip: Inland from the coast, the road corkscrews upward through Lovcen National Park to the Njegos Mausoleum at 1,657 metres. On a clear afternoon, the view from the summit terrace takes in four countries. Budget three hours for the round trip, more if the switchbacks slow you down.
budvaBody.gettingAroundText: Budva's Old Town is pedestrian-only, but the car parks lining the approach road fill fast between June and September. Arrive before 09:00 in high summer or try the less obvious lots south of the main strip near Bečići. A car is not optional for beach-hopping — the riviera's best sand is scattered across fifteen kilometres, and local buses are infrequent.

perastBody.h1: A Republic of Sailors in Miniature
perastBody.p1: Perast occupies a single slender strip of waterfront beneath Mount St Elias, so narrow that most buildings are only one room deep. Roughly 350 people live here year-round, yet this hamlet once supplied the Venetian Republic with its finest sea captains. Between 1420 and 1797, Perast's shipyards launched galleys, its navigators charted the Adriatic, and its merchant families built palazzo after palazzo along the quay. The Venetian lion still decorates lintels and fountain basins throughout the village.
perastBody.p2: Driving from Kotor takes fifteen minutes along the bay road — a route that hugs the waterline so closely you could trail a hand in the Adriatic at some points. Park at the northern edge of the village (spaces are scarce and the single main road is one-way in summer) and walk the entire waterfront in ten minutes. That brevity is deceptive; there is more per metre here than almost anywhere on the coast.
perastBody.stGeorgeText: The natural island closer to shore is cloaked in dark cypresses and closed to the public. A Benedictine abbey has occupied the site since the 12th century. Monks still maintain the grounds, visible as shadowed movement among the trees from the Perast harbourside. Photographers favour early morning, when the island's silhouette is sharpest against the eastern light.
perastBody.ladyOfRocksTitle: Gospa od Škrpjela (Our Lady of the Rocks)
perastBody.ladyOfRocksText: On 22 July 1452 — the date is recorded precisely — two fishermen hauled up an icon of the Madonna from the seabed near a submerged rock. The townspeople began sinking stones around that rock, and the tradition has continued annually for over five centuries. Today the resulting island holds a small baroque church with 68 paintings by Tripo Kokolja. Skiffs depart the harbour every half-hour; the crossing takes five minutes and costs a few euros.
perastBody.historicText: Count the palaces along the waterfront and you reach sixteen. Add seventeen Catholic churches and several guard towers, and you begin to grasp how wealthy this tiny settlement once was. The Museum of Perast occupies the Palazzo Bujović, a building so elegant it is often cited as the finest secular structure on the Montenegrin coast. Maritime instruments, ship models, battle flags, and town records fill its rooms.
perastBody.monasteryText: Continuing along the bay road past Perast toward Risan, a minor turn leads to the Banja Monastery. Religious artefacts from Russian, Greek, and Boka dynasties are stored here in modest surroundings. Few visitors make the detour, which is precisely the appeal.

tivatBody.p1: Until the 1990s, Tivat was a Yugoslav navy town — a closed facility where submarines were serviced and warships provisioned. Visitors were neither expected nor particularly welcome. That history makes the transformation all the more remarkable. Canadian billionaire Peter Munk acquired the decommissioned Arsenal in 2006, and Porto Montenegro was born: 450 berths, a waterfront promenade, and a new identity for the entire town.
tivatBody.p2: For travellers arriving at Tivat Airport (code TIV, 4 km from the town centre and 8 km from Kotor), the town functions as both gateway and destination. Collect a rental car at arrivals and you can be inside Kotor's walls in twenty minutes, or parked at a Tivat beach bar in five. The two towns share the bay but almost nothing else in character.
tivatBody.beachesText: Tivat municipality counts 17 beaches along a surprisingly varied 3 km of coast. Two are particularly worth the drive:
tivatBody.beach1: Plavi Horizonti (Blue Horizons) on the Luštica peninsula: fine white sand in a sheltered cove, water so transparent you can count pebbles at three metres. Reached via a short drive from Tivat centre
tivatBody.beach2: Sveti Marko island beaches near Przno: remnants of a 1960s resort, now half-reclaimed by nature. Boat taxis run from Przno harbour in summer
tivatBody.portoText: The old Arsenal dry docks now shelter superyachts up to 250 metres long. The surrounding waterfront village has been purpose-built with boutiques, a pool club, waterfront restaurants, and a Naval Heritage Collection housed in the original submarine repair buildings. Summer evenings bring open-air events on the quayside. Whether you arrive by car or by 60-metre catamaran, the dress code is the same: relaxed Mediterranean.
tivatBody.monasteryText: On the tip of the Prevlaka peninsula, archaeological digs continue to reveal layers of a 10th-century monastic complex. The site is modest but atmospheric — stone walls, scattered column fragments, and views across the Tivat strait toward Herceg Novi.
tivatBody.churchText: This compact Baroque church on a hillside above Tivat contains religious paintings and silver reliquaries. The real draw is the terrace outside: an elevated panorama stretching from Porto Montenegro's masts to Kotor's fortress walls across the water.
tivatBody.museumText: Kotor's Maritime Museum, a twenty-minute drive away, puts Tivat's naval past in context. Displays cover centuries of Boka seamanship under Venetian and Austro-Hungarian flags — the tradition that ultimately led to a submarine base where luxury yachts now park.

tivatAirportBody.h1: Tivat Airport (TIV): Eight Kilometres to the Fortress Walls
tivatAirportBody.p1: Pilots know Tivat for its approach: aircraft descend between Mount Lovćen and the Vrmac ridge, bank over the bay, and touch down on a runway that practically ends at the waterline. For passengers, the spectacle is the first hint that ordinary geography does not apply here. The terminal (IATA code TIV) sits 4 km from Tivat town centre and 8 km from Kotor's Sea Gate — one of the shortest airport-to-historic-centre distances in southern Europe.
tivatAirportBody.p2: Year-round flights connect to Belgrade, with seasonal charters multiplying the schedule between May and October. Budget carriers have expanded routes in recent years, adding cities like London, Vienna, and Moscow to the summer timetable. A modernised international departures terminal handles the summer surge, though the airport retains the compact feel of a regional hub.
tivatAirportBody.dist1: Kotor Old Town: 8 km — roughly 15 minutes along the bay road
tivatAirportBody.dist2: Perast waterfront: about 25 minutes, continuing north past Kotor
tivatAirportBody.dist3: Budva riviera: around 20 minutes south through the mountain tunnel
tivatAirportBody.dist4: Herceg Novi: approximately 40 minutes via the bay's northern shore
tivatAirportBody.facilitiesText: Expect a cafe, a small duty-free section, souvenir stands, and an ATM. Bus connections exist to Herceg Novi and Budva, but they are infrequent and slow. Taxis queue outside arrivals. Far simpler: have a rental car waiting in the car park, keys handed to you at the gate.
tivatAirportBody.whyText: Tivat is the only airport where Kotor is a fifteen-minute drive. From the moment you leave the terminal, four distinct trips branch outward. North along the bay road to Perast and its man-made island. South through the tunnel to Budva's beaches. Upward on the serpentine to Lovćen National Park's 25 hairpin bends. Or straight ahead, through the outer suburbs of Dobrota, to the car parks beneath Kotor's fortress walls. No shuttle transfers, no waiting for a bus that may or may not appear.
tivatAirportBody.meetText: Our driver waits in the arrivals hall, your name on a board. The car is parked directly outside. From wheels down on the runway to wheels rolling toward Kotor: about fifteen minutes.

podgoricaBody.h1: A Capital Built on Riverbanks and Reinvention
podgoricaBody.p1: Where six rivers converge — the Morača, Ribnica, Zeta, Sitnica, Maréza, and Cijevna — Podgorica has grown into a city of roughly 170,000 people and three overlapping identities. Ottoman Podgorica survives in the Stara Varoš quarter: narrow lanes, a squat clock tower (Sahat-kula), and low stone houses. Yugoslav Podgorica sprawls in the concrete apartment blocks of Blok V and the wide boulevards renamed after independence. Modern Podgorica rises in the glass-and-steel towers near the Millennium Bridge, a cable-stayed span that has become the city's unofficial logo.
podgoricaBody.p2: From Kotor, the drive takes about 90 minutes. Two routes exist: the dramatic Lovćen serpentine, climbing 25 hairpin bends before descending through Cetinje to the Zeta plain, or the faster motorway-standard road via Budva and Sozina tunnel. Both end at a city that few coastal tourists bother to visit — their loss.
podgoricaBody.p3: Podgorica is nobody's picture-postcard destination, and it makes no apologies. Instead it offers the best restaurants in Montenegro, a lively cafe culture along the Morača riverbank, and an emerging gallery scene concentrated around the Centre for Contemporary Art. It is also the logical launching point for Lake Skadar, the Morača canyon, and the mountain north.
podgoricaBody.seeText: The archaeological ruins of Duklja (Roman Doclea), two thousand years old, lie on the northern outskirts. In the city centre, the Cathedral of the Resurrection of Christ — consecrated in 2013 after two decades of construction — dominates the skyline with its massive dome. For something less monumental, the Petrović Palace on Kruševac hill houses a modest but well-curated art gallery and pleasant wooded grounds.
podgoricaBody.skadarText: Thirty minutes south of Podgorica, the largest lake in the Balkans straddles the Albanian border. Pelicans, cormorants, and herons crowd the wetlands. Hire a small boat at Virpazar village, paddle among the water lilies, and return for grilled carp at one of the lakeside restaurants. In spring, the lake level rises by several metres and entire meadows vanish underwater.
podgoricaBody.danilovgradText: Half an hour west along the Zeta river, Danilovgrad is a small town known for riverside swimming spots, kayak rentals, and the 13th-century Zdrebaonik Monastery tucked into the hillside above. Combine it with a stop at one of the valley's family-run wineries.
podgoricaBody.zabljak: The mountain town of Žabljak, at 1,456 metres in the Durmitor massif, is 170 km and roughly 2.5 hours north. In winter: skiing. In summer: the Tara River canyon (1,300 metres deep, second only to the Grand Canyon), Black Lake, and hiking trails across a UNESCO-protected alpine plateau.

podgoricaAirportBody.p1: The three-letter code TGD is a fossil. It dates from when Podgorica was still called Titograd, before Montenegro's capital reclaimed its older name in 1992. The airport, however, has moved firmly into the present: a terminal rebuilt in 2006, year-round connections to a dozen European capitals, and the widest winter flight network in Montenegro. If Tivat is the summer shortcut to Kotor (8 km away), Podgorica is the reliable alternative when seasonal routes shut down.
podgoricaAirportBody.p2: The original terminal building now houses a small collection on Montenegrin aviation history — worth a glance if your flight is delayed. The new terminal is functional and compact: eight departure gates, two arrival gates, and none of the sprawl of a major hub.
podgoricaAirportBody.distIntro: Podgorica Airport sits 12 km south of the city centre, on the road toward Lake Skadar.
podgoricaAirportBody.dist1: Podgorica city centre: 12 km, around 15 minutes by car (taxi roughly 10 euros)
podgoricaAirportBody.dist2: Kotor via the mountain road: approximately 90 minutes, 100 km
podgoricaAirportBody.dist3: Budva via the Sozina tunnel: approximately 60 minutes
podgoricaAirportBody.dist4: Cetinje, the old royal capital: approximately 30 minutes
podgoricaAirportBody.flightsText: Belgrade is the anchor route, operating daily year-round. Regular services reach Paris, Rome, Frankfurt, Vienna, Budapest, Ljubljana, and several Eastern European cities. Summer charters add capacity, but unlike Tivat, Podgorica never goes quiet in winter — a meaningful advantage for off-season travellers.
podgoricaAirportBody.facilitiesText: Inside: a cafe, a duty-free shop, an ATM, and car rental counters. Outside: 25,000 square metres of parking. The airport is small enough that clearing arrivals and reaching the car park takes minutes, not the marathon trudge familiar from larger hubs.
podgoricaAirportBody.awardText: Podgorica Airport holds the ACI Europe Best Airport Award in the under-one-million-passengers category — recognition of efficient handling in a compact footprint.
podgoricaAirportBody.rentalText: A driver meets you at the arrivals gate, keys ready. No shuttle rides, no queues at a distant lot. Within minutes of stepping off the plane, you can be heading south on the mountain road toward Kotor. The 90-minute drive crosses the Lovćen massif via Cetinje, with views that justify every hairpin bend.

dubrovnikAirportBody.p1: Dubrovnik's flight network dwarfs both Montenegrin airports combined. Direct routes reach most major European cities year-round, with low-cost carriers adding dozens more in summer. The catch: Dubrovnik is in Croatia, and Kotor is in Montenegro. The reward: the two-hour drive between them follows the E65 along one of the most celebrated coastlines in the Mediterranean.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p2: After collecting your car at DBV, the road heads south-east through the Croatian Konavle valley before reaching the border checkpoint at Debeli Brijeg. Once through, the highway descends into Herceg Novi, crosses the entrance to the Bay of Kotor, and traces the northern shoreline past Perast all the way to Kotor's walls. It is not a fast road — it is an experience in itself.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p3: A Green Card (international motor insurance certificate) must be in the glovebox before you cross. We arrange this at booking. Confirm it covers Montenegro when you reserve, and there is nothing else to worry about at the border except showing your passport.
dubrovnikAirportBody.borderText: In July and August, expect waits of one to two hours at the Croatia-Montenegro border on the coastal E65. Weekday mornings before 08:00 and late evenings after 20:00 are the lightest periods. If your flight arrives mid-afternoon in peak season, factor the delay into your plans. An alternative: the inland crossing at Konfin near Trebinje, which is quieter but adds driving time.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText1: The terminal sits in Čilipi, roughly 25 minutes south-east of Dubrovnik's Old Town. A single runway handles everything from budget turbo-props to wide-body charters. Parking fills quickly in high season; pre-booking a space saves both time and money.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText2: Inside: a bureau de change, bank, post office, duty-free store, several cafes, and a newsstand. Outside: taxi ranks, an Atlas bus connection to the city, and rows of rental car company desks. The airport is functional rather than luxurious — designed to process summer volumes efficiently.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText1: Allow two hours from the airport to Kotor, plus whatever the border adds. The E65 descends from the Croatian plateau to the coast at Herceg Novi, then follows the bay's northern arm eastward. At Kamenari, a short car ferry crosses the strait (saving a long loop around the inner bay via Tivat), or you can continue on the road through Perast and approach Kotor from the north.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText2: Whichever route you take, the final approach to Kotor is unforgettable. Mountains rise vertically from the water, the bay narrows to a channel, and the fortress walls appear clinging to the cliff face ahead. There is no grander way to arrive.

montenegroBody.h1: Driving Montenegro from a Kotor Base
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro is roughly the size of Northern Ireland yet contains a coast, a deep fjord-like bay, a 2,500-year-old walled town, a 1,300-metre-deep canyon, and alpine peaks above 2,500 metres. Driving distances look short on a map. In practice, the roads twist, climb, tunnel, and ferry-cross their way between these extremes, and journey times rarely match what Google predicts. Accept that, and the driving itself becomes part of the holiday.
montenegroBody.p2: Kotor sits at the geographical heart of the action. Perast is fifteen minutes north. Budva is thirty minutes south. Lovćen's summit is an hour of switchbacks above. Lake Skadar is ninety minutes inland. Only Durmitor, in the far north, requires a genuine road trip. Everything else radiates outward from the fortress walls like spokes from a hub.
montenegroBody.startText: Tivat Airport, 8 km from Kotor, is the obvious collection point — fifteen minutes from landing to the Old Town. Podgorica Airport offers wider winter schedules and a scenic 90-minute drive through the mountains. Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia has the most international routes and connects to Kotor via a two-hour coastal road with one border crossing. All three have meet-and-greet handovers.
montenegroBody.timesText1: Perast: 15 min. Tivat: 20 min. Budva: 30 min. Herceg Novi: 45 min. Cetinje: 60 min. Podgorica: 90 min. Dubrovnik: 2 h. Žabljak (Durmitor): 3.5–4 h.
montenegroBody.timesText2: The Kotor–Lovćen serpentine alone contains 25 documented hairpin bends in less than 20 km. Navigation apps routinely underestimate mountain stretches by 20–30%. Plan accordingly and carry water.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Random roadside checks are common, especially on the bay road and near border crossings. Officers will ask for:
montenegroBody.rule1: A valid driving licence (international driving permit accepted alongside it)
montenegroBody.rule2: The original rental contract (photocopies are not sufficient)
montenegroBody.rule3: Proof of insurance (the rental company provides this in the vehicle)
montenegroBody.rule4: A Green Card if crossing any border (approximately 15 euros for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts: mandatory for every occupant, front and rear
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Mobile phones: hands-free only, and even then police may stop you
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Alcohol: zero-tolerance policy — any detectable blood alcohol means an on-the-spot fine or worse
montenegroBody.keyRule4: Speed cameras: fixed units on the bay road and mobile traps on the highway. Fines are issued to the rental company and charged back to your card
montenegroBody.conditionsTitle: What the Roads Are Actually Like
montenegroBody.conditionsText: The bay road between Kotor and Perast is paved and maintained but barely two cars wide in places, with stone walls on one side and the water on the other. Mountain roads above 800 metres may lack guard rails entirely. The Sozina tunnel to Budva is modern and well-lit. The Lovćen road is narrow, steep, and has limited passing places — honk before blind bends. In winter, chains or winter tyres are legally required above the snow line.
montenegroBody.e65Text: This route traces the entire Bay of Kotor from Herceg Novi around the northern arm through Perast and Kotor, then south via Tivat toward Budva. Alternatively, the Kamenari–Lepetane car ferry shortcuts the loop, saving 45 minutes. The bay road is where most visitors spend their driving time, and for good reason — the views change with every headland.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Cuts inland from the Adriatic through the Montenegrin heartland toward the Serbian and eastern Bosnian borders. This is the route to Nikšić, the Piva canyon, and eventually Durmitor. Less scenic near the coast but increasingly dramatic as it climbs.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border driving is permitted with a Green Card. The Croatia crossing at Debeli Brijeg is the busiest — in July and August, waits of one to two hours are normal. Weekday mornings before 08:00 or evenings after 20:00 cut the queue significantly. Other crossings (Albania, Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are generally faster.
montenegroBody.nationText: Independent only since 2006, Montenegro remains lightly visited compared to Croatia or Greece. The roads are quieter, the parking easier, and the landscapes more concentrated. A single tank of fuel can take you from Kotor's Venetian alleyways to a glacial mountain lake and back. Few countries this small pack this much into their borders.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Taking a Rental Car Across Balkan Borders
borderCrossingBody.p1: Montenegro shares land borders with five countries: Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. Driving a rental car into any of them is permitted — provided you tell the rental company at booking so the paperwork is ready when you collect the keys.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card (Carte Internationale d'Assurance Automobile) is a standardised document proving your vehicle carries valid third-party insurance in the country you are entering. Border guards ask for it at every Balkan checkpoint. Without one, you will be turned around.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard1: Typical cost: around 15 euros for a 15-day period
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Issued by: the rental company, at no hassle to you — just confirm the countries you plan to visit when booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Standard coverage: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia. Kosovo may need to be added separately
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Before setting off, open the glovebox and verify the Green Card lists every country on your route. A card valid for Croatia alone will not get you into Bosnia. Five minutes of checking saves hours of frustration at the checkpoint.
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Passport or EU national ID card (must be original, not a photocopy)
borderCrossingBody.doc2: Driving licence — an international driving permit is recommended alongside non-Latin-script licences
borderCrossingBody.doc3: Original rental contract with company stamp (printed, not a screenshot on your phone)
borderCrossingBody.doc4: Green Card insurance certificate listing all destination countries
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration certificate (kept in the car by the rental company)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The E65 coastal crossing between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik is by far the most popular — and therefore the most congested. In July and August, two passport checks (Montenegrin exit, Croatian entry) combine with heavy tourist traffic to produce queues of one to two hours. The crossing itself is a simple road checkpoint with booths on both sides.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Timing matters enormously. Cross before 08:00 or after 20:00 and the wait drops to minutes. Weekday mornings are dramatically quieter than Saturday or Sunday. If you must cross mid-afternoon in August, bring water and patience.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: Heading north from Kotor, the Kamenari–Lepetane car ferry crosses the narrowest point of the bay in about five minutes, saving a 45-minute drive around the inner shore via Tivat. Ferries run continuously; the fare is a few euros.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaTitle: Into Albania: Sukobin or Hani i Hotit
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: The coastal crossing at Sukobin/Muriqan, south of Ulcinj, is the more straightforward route — well-signed, with generally short queues. The inland alternative at Hani i Hotit, near the southern shore of Lake Skadar, is scenic but quieter and less familiar to foreign tourists.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Albanian road infrastructure is improving rapidly but remains unpredictable outside main highways. Speed limits drop on rural roads and surface quality can change without warning. Drive defensively, particularly at night.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaTitle: Into Bosnia: Šćepan Polje or Vilusi
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: Heading north from the coast, the Šćepan Polje crossing near Foča accesses the Tara River gorge and Sutjeska National Park. From Nikšić, the Vilusi crossing provides a route toward Trebinje and eventually Mostar. Both are lightly used and rarely involve waiting.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: The Dobrakovo checkpoint on the E65, north of Bijelo Polje, connects to the Serbian motorway network. From there, Belgrade is roughly four hours of straightforward motorway driving. Expect a vignette (road-use toll) on the Serbian side.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The Kula crossing near Rožaje leads to Peja/Peć in western Kosovo. Confirm with your rental company that Kosovo is explicitly listed on your Green Card — several insurers exclude it by default, and border guards will check.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Arrange documents in a single envelope on the dashboard before joining the queue. Fumbling at the window wastes time
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Switch off the engine while stationary — guards notice and it puts them at ease
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Avoid the Debeli Brijeg crossing on Saturday and Sunday afternoons between June and September
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Inland crossings (Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are almost always faster than the coastal Croatian one
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Carry euros in cash. Croatian motorway tolls accept cards, but Bosnian and Albanian fuel stations sometimes do not
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check your mobile roaming plan before crossing. Data charges spike the moment your phone connects to a foreign network
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Traffic Laws That Change at the Border
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Regulations differ from one country to the next. The main surprises:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: daytime running lights mandatory at all times. Motorway tolls are collected by cash or card at barrier stations
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on secondary roads than in Montenegro. Livestock on the carriageway is not uncommon outside towns
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres compulsory from November through April. A first-aid kit and warning triangle must be in the vehicle
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: absolute zero-tolerance alcohol limit for drivers. A motorway vignette must be purchased at the border or a fuel station
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: right-hand traffic, same as Montenegro. An international driving permit is strongly recommended
borderCrossingBody.route1: Kotor to Dubrovnik: 2 hours on the coastal E65, one border, and arguably the finest short coastal drive in the Mediterranean
borderCrossingBody.route2: Kotor to Mostar: 3–4 hours via Trebinje. The Stari Most bridge and Ottoman quarter are the draw
borderCrossingBody.route3: Kotor to Shkodër: roughly 3 hours through Podgorica and along Lake Skadar. A different country, a different pace entirely
borderCrossingBody.route4: Kotor to Belgrade: 6–7 hours via Podgorica and the E65 north. Motorway most of the way on the Serbian side
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Looking for a car that comes with cross-border paperwork already sorted? Search our fleet, tick the countries you plan to visit at booking, and the Green Card will be in the glovebox when you collect the keys.
